title: Health and the Body
description: Talk about how you feel: body parts (la cabeza, la garganta, la espalda...), symptoms with DOLER (which works like gustar) and tener (tener fiebre, gripe), a visit to the doctor or pharmacy, and giving simple advice with deber / tener que and gentle commands.

cards.front: la cabeza, el estómago, la garganta, la espalda
cards.back: the head, the stomach, the throat, the back
cards.notes: Core body parts. Note the genders: **la** cabeza, **el** estómago, **la** garganta, **la** espalda. *Estómago* carries an accent on the first **ó**.

cards.front: el brazo, la pierna, el pie, la mano
cards.back: the arm, the leg, the foot, the hand
cards.notes: *La mano* is feminine even though it ends in **-o** — a classic exception: *la mano derecha* (the right hand). Plurals: *los pies*, *las manos*.

cards.back: the teeth (sg. el diente = the tooth)
cards.notes: Singular **el diente**, plural **los dientes**. *Me duelen los dientes* = my teeth hurt (plural, so *duelen*).

cards.front: Me duele la cabeza. / Me duelen las piernas.
cards.back: My head hurts. / My legs hurt. (doler = to hurt)
cards.notes: **Doler works like gustar.** The body part is the *subject*: *duele* with one part, *duelen* with several. Literally 'the head hurts to me'. Use the **definite article** (la cabeza), never *mi cabeza*. Never say *Yo duelo*.
Change the pronoun to change *whose* part hurts: *Me duele* (it hurts me), *Te duele* (it hurts you), *Le duele* (it hurts him/her). The verb still agrees with the body part, not the person.

cards.front: tener fiebre, tener tos, tener gripe
cards.back: to have a fever, to have a cough, to have the flu
cards.notes: Many symptoms use **tener** (no article): *Tengo fiebre / tos / gripe*. Also *tener dolor de...*: *Tengo dolor de cabeza* = I have a headache.

cards.back: I feel ill / I don't feel well.
cards.notes: **Encontrarse** is reflexive here ('to feel'): *me encuentro, te encuentras, se encuentra*. *Me encuentro mal* = I feel unwell; *Me encuentro mejor* = I feel better.

cards.front: el médico, la farmacia, la receta
cards.back: the doctor, the pharmacy, the prescription
cards.notes: *El médico / la médica* = the doctor. *La farmacia* = pharmacy. *La receta* means both 'prescription' and 'recipe' — context decides.
*Las pastillas* = pills/tablets; *el jarabe* = syrup. *Tómate las pastillas* = take the pills.

cards.back: What's wrong (with you)? / What's the matter?
cards.notes: The doctor uses the formal *¿Qué **le** pasa?* (with *usted*); friends use *¿Qué **te** pasa?* Answer with *Me duele...* or *Me encuentro mal*.

cards.front: Debes descansar. / Tienes que beber agua.
cards.back: You should rest. / You have to drink water.
cards.notes: Give advice with **deber** + infinitive (*debes descansar* = you should rest) or **tener que** + infinitive (*tienes que beber agua* = you have to drink water). The second verb stays in the infinitive.

cards.front: Tómate esto. / Ve al médico. / Descansa.
cards.back: Take this. / Go to the doctor. / Rest.
cards.notes: Gentle commands (tú imperative): *descansa* (rest), *ve al médico* (go to the doctor), *tómate esto* (take this — *toma* + reflexive *te*). These are everyday, friendly suggestions.

steps.title: The body and how you feel
steps.body:
# Health and the body

Let's learn to say what hurts and how we feel. First, the **body parts** — and notice the article and gender of each:

| Spanish | English |
|---|---|
| la cabeza | the head |
| el estómago | the stomach |
| la garganta | the throat |
| la espalda | the back |
| el brazo | the arm |
| la pierna | the leg |
| el pie | the foot |
| la mano | the hand |
| los dientes | the teeth |

> ⚠️ **la mano** is feminine even though it ends in **-o**.

To say a symptom, two patterns are common:

- **tener** + symptom: *Tengo **fiebre** / **tos** / **gripe*** (I have a fever / cough / the flu).
- **tener dolor de** + part: *Tengo **dolor de cabeza*** (I have a headache).

And to say how you feel in general, use the reflexive **encontrarse**: *Me encuentro **mal*** (I feel ill), *Me encuentro **mejor*** (I feel better).

steps.title: DOLER works like GUSTAR
steps.body:
## Saying what hurts: doler

The verb **doler** (to hurt) does **not** work like English *to hurt*. It works exactly like **gustar**.

The **body part is the subject**, and the person is an indirect-object pronoun (*me, te, le...*). Literally you say *the head hurts **to me***:

> **Me duele la cabeza.** = My head hurts.
> **Me duelen las piernas.** = My legs hurt.

The verb agrees with the **body part**, not the person:

| | one part | several parts |
|---|---|---|
| me (to me) | me **duele** la espalda | me **duelen** los pies |
| te (to you) | te **duele** la garganta | te **duelen** las manos |
| le (to him/her) | le **duele** el estómago | le **duelen** los dientes |

Two common English-speaker mistakes:

> ❌ **Yo duelo la cabeza.** — *doler* is never conjugated to match *yo*. The head is the subject, so it's *Me **duele** la cabeza*.

> ❌ **Me duele mi cabeza.** — use the **definite article**, not a possessive: *Me duele **la** cabeza*. The pronoun *me* already tells us whose head it is.

There is also *tener dolor de...* (*Tengo dolor de cabeza*), but never mix the two: don't say *Tengo duele*.

steps.title: At the doctor and giving advice
steps.body:
## At the doctor or pharmacy

Useful words: **el médico** (the doctor), **la farmacia** (the pharmacy), **la receta** (the prescription), **las pastillas** (pills), **el jarabe** (syrup).

The doctor asks (formally, with *usted*): **¿Qué le pasa?** (What's wrong?). A friend asks: **¿Qué te pasa?** You answer with *Me duele...* or **Me encuentro mal** (I feel ill).

## Giving advice

Three easy ways to suggest what someone should do:

- **deber** + infinitive — a soft 'should': *Debes **descansar*** (You should rest).
- **tener que** + infinitive — a stronger 'have to': *Tienes que **beber** agua* (You have to drink water).
- gentle **commands** (tú imperative): *Descansa* (Rest), *Ve al médico* (Go to the doctor), *Tómate esto* (Take this).

Notice the second verb after *deber* and *tener que* stays in the **infinitive**: *Debes **ir** al médico*, not *Debes vas*.

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish body part with its English meaning.

exercise.hint: doler works like gustar: the head is the subject. Use the article la, not mi.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the correct form of *doler*. The body part is plural!
exercise.hint: doler agrees with the body part. 'las piernas' is plural, so the verb is plural too.

steps.title: Build: I have a sore throat

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words to say: *My throat hurts.*
exercise.hint: doler + la garganta (singular, so duele). Start with the pronoun Me.

exercise.prompt: Fill in the verb to say 'I have a fever'.
exercise.hint: Symptoms use tener: yo form = tengo. No article before fiebre.

exercise.prompt: You want to tell the doctor 'I feel ill'. Which sentence is correct?
exercise.hint: encontrarse is reflexive: me encuentro. Use the adverb mal (badly), not malo.

exercise.prompt: Match each Spanish word with its English meaning.

accept: A él le duele la espalda.
exercise.hint: Use le (to him) + duele (one part) + la espalda. Use the article, not su.

exercise.prompt: Arrange the words for a gentle command: *Take this and rest.*
The doctor (using usted) asks what is wrong. Fill in the pronoun.
exercise.hint: Formal 'you' (usted) uses the pronoun le: ¿Qué le pasa?

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I have a fever. You have to go to the doctor.* (tener fiebre; tener que)
accept: Tengo fiebre. Tienes que ir al médico.
tengo fiebre tienes que ir al médico
Tengo fiebre. Tienes que ir al medico.
Tengo fiebre. Tienes que ir al médico
exercise.hint: Tengo fiebre (tener, no article). Then tienes que + infinitive ir + al médico.
distractors: Tengo fiebre. Tienes que vas al médico.
Tengo la fiebre. Tienes que ir al médico.
Tengo fiebre. Debes ir el médico.
